reasoning: I will provide a concise, patient-friendly summary of the main and less common causes of hypoglycemia, plus practical steps for immediate management and when to seek care.
output:
Hypoglycemia is a blood sugar drop, usually from too much diabetes medicine or not eating enough, but many other causes exist.

- Main cause/mechanism: too much insulin or diabetes meds, or not eating enough after taking them (and sometimes more exercise).

- Other common causes (in people without diabetes): drinking a lot of alcohol, liver or kidney disease, long periods without eating (starvation), tumors that make too much insulin (insulinoma) or other hormone disorders.

- After-meal cases: reactive or postprandial hypoglycemia can occur when your body releases too much insulin after eating, sometimes after stomach surgery.

- Symptoms to watch: shakiness, sweating, hunger, irritability; in more severe cases, confusion, seizures, or loss of consciousness.

- Immediate management and next steps: start with 15–20 g of fast-acting carbs (glucose tablet, juice, regular soda) and recheck in 15 minutes; repeat if still below 70 mg/dL, then eat a snack to stabilize. If you can’t swallow or symptoms are severe, use glucagon or IV glucose as directed by a clinician. See a doctor to identify the underlying cause and adjust treatment if needed.

- Important caveat: non-diabetic causes are less common; persistent or recurrent episodes require medical evaluation; consult a clinician to determine the exact cause and appropriate treatment.

- Practical next step: if you have diabetes, discuss with your healthcare provider about adjusting meds; if you don’t have diabetes and have recurrent symptoms, seek medical evaluation promptly; seek urgent care if there is severe symptoms or unconsciousness.

This information is general; individual risk and decisions depend on specific circumstances and should be discussed with a qualified professional.
